questions.question: How do you write unit tests in .NET with xUnit? What makes a good unit test?
questions.answer:
Unit testing is something I take seriously — not just to satisfy a checklist, but because it's genuinely caught more bugs than code review has in my experience. xUnit is my go-to framework; it's what the .NET team uses internally and has great async support.

xUnit basics:

// Install: xunit, xunit.runner.visualstudio, Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk

public class OrderServiceTests
{
    // [Fact] = single test case
    [Fact]
    public void CalculateTotal_WithTwoItems_ReturnsSumOfPrices()
    {
        // Arrange -- set up the scenario
        var items = new List<OrderItem>
        {
            new OrderItem { Quantity = 2, UnitPrice = 10.00m },
            new OrderItem { Quantity = 1, UnitPrice = 25.00m }
        };

        // Act -- call the thing you're testing
        var total = OrderCalculator.CalculateTotal(items);

        // Assert -- verify the result
        Assert.Equal(45.00m, total);
    }

    // [Theory] + [InlineData] = parameterised test, runs once per data row
    [Theory]
    [InlineData(0, false)]
    [InlineData(-1, false)]
    [InlineData(1, true)]
    [InlineData(100, true)]
    public void IsValidQuantity_ReturnsExpectedResult(int quantity, bool expected)
    {
        var result = OrderValidator.IsValidQuantity(quantity);
        Assert.Equal(expected, result);
    }

    // Testing exceptions
    [Fact]
    public void PlaceOrder_WithEmptyCart_ThrowsInvalidOperationException()
    {
        var service = new OrderService(/* deps */);
        Assert.Throws<InvalidOperationException>(
            () => service.PlaceOrder(new Cart { Items = new() }));
    }

    // Async tests
    [Fact]
    public async Task GetOrderAsync_WithValidId_ReturnsOrder()
    {
        // Arrange
        var mockRepo = new Mock<IOrderRepository>();
        mockRepo.Setup(r => r.GetByIdAsync(1))
                .ReturnsAsync(new Order { Id = 1, Status = "Pending" });

        var service = new OrderService(mockRepo.Object);

        // Act
        var order = await service.GetOrderAsync(1);

        // Assert
        Assert.NotNull(order);
        Assert.Equal("Pending", order.Status);
    }
}

What makes a good unit test:

  • AAA structure — Arrange, Act, Assert. Every test follows this structure.
  • One assertion per test (roughly) — when a test fails, you know exactly what broke.
  • Descriptive name — MethodName_Scenario_ExpectedResult. I can read the test name and know what it tests without reading the body.
  • Fast — unit tests should run in milliseconds. No real DB, no HTTP calls, no file I/O — mock those away.
  • Independent — tests don't share state, don't depend on run order.
  • Tests the contract, not the implementation — test what the method returns/does, not how it does it internally.

followUps.question: What is the difference between a Fact and a Theory in xUnit?
followUps.answer:
[Fact] is a test that always has the same input — no parameters. One test case. [Theory] is a parameterised test driven by data. Combined with [InlineData], [MemberData], or [ClassData], xUnit runs the test method once per data row. This avoids writing the same test logic 10 times for 10 different inputs.

[Theory]
[InlineData("test@email.com", true)]
[InlineData("notanemail", false)]
[InlineData("", false)]
[InlineData(null, false)]
public void IsValidEmail_ReturnsExpectedResult(string email, bool expected)
{
    Assert.Equal(expected, EmailValidator.IsValid(email));
}
// Generates 4 separate test cases in Test Explorer

questions.tip: Name your tests like documentation. If I have to read the test body to understand what it tests, the name is too vague. 'Test1()' tells me nothing. 'CalculateDiscount_PremiumUserOver100_Returns20PercentDiscount()' tells me everything.

questions.question: How do you use Moq for mocking in unit tests? When should you mock vs not mock?
questions.answer:
Moq is a mocking framework that creates fake implementations of interfaces at runtime, so you can test a class in isolation without needing its real dependencies. I use it on almost every unit test that involves a service with external dependencies.

Core Moq usage:

// Install: Moq

public class OrderServiceTests
{
    private readonly Mock<IOrderRepository> _mockRepo;
    private readonly Mock<IEmailService> _mockEmail;
    private readonly Mock<ILogger<OrderService>> _mockLogger;
    private readonly OrderService _sut; // SUT = System Under Test

    public OrderServiceTests()
    {
        _mockRepo  = new Mock<IOrderRepository>();
        _mockEmail = new Mock<IEmailService>();
        _mockLogger = new Mock<ILogger<OrderService>>();
        _sut = new OrderService(_mockRepo.Object, _mockEmail.Object, _mockLogger.Object);
    }

    [Fact]
    public async Task PlaceOrderAsync_ValidOrder_SavesAndSendsEmail()
    {
        // Setup -- define what mocks return
        var cart = new Cart { UserId = 1, Items = new() { new CartItem { ProductId = 5, Qty = 2 } } };
        _mockRepo.Setup(r => r.SaveOrderAsync(It.IsAny<Order>()))
                 .ReturnsAsync(new Order { Id = 99 });

        // Act
        var orderId = await _sut.PlaceOrderAsync(cart);

        // Assert the return value
        Assert.Equal(99, orderId);

        // Verify method was called -- did we actually send the email?
        _mockEmail.Verify(
            e => e.SendAsync(
                It.Is<string>(s => s.Contains("order")), // partial match on subject
                It.IsAny<string>()),
            Times.Once);

        // Verify repo was called with correct data
        _mockRepo.Verify(
            r => r.SaveOrderAsync(It.Is<Order>(o => o.UserId == 1)),
            Times.Once);
    }

    [Fact]
    public async Task PlaceOrderAsync_RepoThrows_DoesNotSendEmail()
    {
        _mockRepo.Setup(r => r.SaveOrderAsync(It.IsAny<Order>()))
                 .ThrowsAsync(new Exception("DB error"));

        await Assert.ThrowsAsync<Exception>(() => _sut.PlaceOrderAsync(new Cart()));

        // Email should NOT be sent if save failed
        _mockEmail.Verify(
            e => e.SendAsync(It.IsAny<string>(), It.IsAny<string>()),
            Times.Never);
    }
}

When to mock vs not mock:

  • Mock: external dependencies — databases, HTTP clients, email services, file system, clocks (IDateTime), anything that has side effects or is slow.
  • Don't mock: the class under test itself, simple value objects/DTOs, pure functions with no dependencies, things that are cheap and deterministic (like a string parser).
  • Don't mock EF Core DbContext directly — it's painful. Instead, use an InMemory provider or SQLite in-memory for tests that need to verify EF queries. Or abstract behind a repository interface and mock the repository.

MockBehavior — useful for strict mocking:

// Strict mode -- any call not set up throws MockException
// Forces you to be explicit about every call the SUT makes
var mock = new Mock<IOrderRepository>(MockBehavior.Strict);
// Default is Loose -- unset calls return default values

followUps.question: What is the difference between a Mock, a Stub, and a Fake?
followUps.answer:
These terms are often used interchangeably but have precise meanings:

  • Stub — returns canned data. You set it up to return a specific value. No verification of how it was called. Moq's Setup().Returns() creates a stub.
  • Mock — a stub that also records interactions, allowing you to verify it was called correctly. Moq's Verify() makes it a mock. If you're not calling Verify, you're using Moq as a stub.
  • Fake — a working implementation that takes shortcuts unsuitable for production. Example: an in-memory repository that implements IOrderRepository using a List<Order>. Faster to write integration-style tests with. The EF Core InMemory provider is a Fake.
  • Spy — wraps a real object and records calls to it. Rarely needed in .NET.

questions.tip: Don't over-mock. If you're mocking 5 dependencies to test one class, your class has too many dependencies (violates SRP). Mocking is a smell detector — lots of mocks in a test = class doing too much.

questions.question: How do you write integration tests in ASP.NET Core? What is WebApplicationFactory?
questions.answer:
Integration tests verify that multiple components work together — the controller, service, EF Core, middleware, and routing all wired up correctly. I write far fewer integration tests than unit tests, but they catch a different class of bugs that unit tests miss (like a misconfigured DI registration or a wrong route).

WebApplicationFactory — spin up your real app in tests

// Install: Microsoft.AspNetCore.Mvc.Testing

// A shared test fixture — creates one instance of the app for the whole test class
public class OrderApiTests : IClassFixture<WebApplicationFactory<Program>>
{
    private readonly HttpClient _client;

    public OrderApiTests(WebApplicationFactory<Program> factory)
    {
        _client = factory
            .WithWebHostBuilder(builder =>
            {
                builder.ConfigureServices(services =>
                {
                    // Replace real DB with in-memory DB
                    var dbDescriptor = services.SingleOrDefault(
                        d => d.ServiceType == typeof(DbContextOptions<AppDbContext>));
                    if (dbDescriptor != null) services.Remove(dbDescriptor);

                    services.AddDbContext<AppDbContext>(opts =>
                        opts.UseInMemoryDatabase("TestDb"));

                    // Replace real email service with fake
                    services.AddSingleton<IEmailService, FakeEmailService>();
                });
            })
            .CreateClient();
    }

    [Fact]
    public async Task GetOrder_ValidId_Returns200WithOrder()
    {
        // Arrange -- seed data
        // ... use a scope to add test data to in-memory DB

        // Act -- real HTTP request through the real ASP.NET pipeline
        var response = await _client.GetAsync("/api/orders/1");

        // Assert
        response.EnsureSuccessStatusCode();
        var order = await response.Content.ReadFromJsonAsync<OrderDto>();
        Assert.NotNull(order);
        Assert.Equal(1, order.Id);
    }

    [Fact]
    public async Task CreateOrder_InvalidModel_Returns400()
    {
        var dto = new CreateOrderDto { }; // missing required fields
        var response = await _client.PostAsJsonAsync("/api/orders", dto);
        Assert.Equal(HttpStatusCode.BadRequest, response.StatusCode);
    }

    [Fact]
    public async Task GetOrders_Unauthenticated_Returns401()
    {
        var response = await _client.GetAsync("/api/orders");
        Assert.Equal(HttpStatusCode.Unauthorized, response.StatusCode);
    }
}

// Custom factory for authenticated requests
public class AuthenticatedApiTests : IClassFixture<WebApplicationFactory<Program>>
{
    private readonly HttpClient _client;

    public AuthenticatedApiTests(WebApplicationFactory<Program> factory)
    {
        _client = factory.CreateClient();
        // Add JWT token for all requests
        var token = TestJwtGenerator.Generate(userId: 1, roles: new[] { "Admin" });
        _client.DefaultRequestHeaders.Authorization =
            new AuthenticationHeaderValue("Bearer", token);
    }
}

SQLite in-memory for more realistic DB tests:

// Better than EF InMemory -- actually runs SQL, catches constraint violations etc.
services.AddDbContext<AppDbContext>(opts =>
    opts.UseSqlite("DataSource=:memory:"));

// Must open the connection and create schema
var sp = services.BuildServiceProvider();
using var scope = sp.CreateScope();
var db = scope.ServiceProvider.GetRequiredService<AppDbContext>();
db.Database.OpenConnection();
db.Database.EnsureCreated();

followUps.question: What is the difference between unit tests, integration tests, and end-to-end tests?
followUps.answer:
The testing pyramid model: lots of unit tests (base), fewer integration tests (middle), minimal E2E tests (top).

  • Unit tests — test a single class/method in isolation. Fast (ms), no external dependencies (mock everything). Catch logic bugs. Run on every save in your IDE.
  • Integration tests — test multiple components working together. May use a real DB (in-memory or SQLite), real middleware pipeline. Slower (seconds). Catch wiring/configuration bugs. Run in CI on every PR.
  • End-to-end (E2E) tests — test the entire system including frontend, via a real browser (Playwright, Selenium). Slowest (minutes). Catch user-facing bugs. Run in CI before production deployment only.

The reason the pyramid has fewer E2E tests at the top: they're slow, flaky, hard to maintain, and expensive to run. Unit tests give 80% of the value at 5% of the cost. The right ratio for most projects: many unit tests, some integration tests for critical flows, a handful of E2E smoke tests.

questions.tip: IClassFixture vs collection fixtures: IClassFixture creates one app instance per test class. If you have multiple test classes that all need the same app, use a collection fixture — it's shared across all classes in the collection, speeding up your test suite significantly.

questions.question: What is Test-Driven Development (TDD)? Do you practice it and when is it useful?
questions.answer:
TDD is writing the test before writing the code. The cycle is: Red (write a failing test) → Green (write minimum code to pass it) → Refactor (clean up). I'm going to be honest here: I don't do strict TDD for every line of code — very few experienced developers do. But I use it deliberately for specific scenarios.

The TDD cycle in practice:

// STEP 1: RED -- write a failing test first
[Fact]
public void ApplyDiscount_PremiumUserOver100_Returns20PercentOff()
{
    var calculator = new PricingCalculator();
    var user = new User { IsPremium = true };
    var price = calculator.ApplyDiscount(user, 120m);
    Assert.Equal(96m, price); // 120 - 20% = 96
    // Test fails -- PricingCalculator doesn't exist yet
}

// STEP 2: GREEN -- minimum code to pass the test
public class PricingCalculator
{
    public decimal ApplyDiscount(User user, decimal price)
    {
        if (user.IsPremium && price > 100)
            return price * 0.80m;
        return price;
    }
}
// Test passes

// STEP 3: REFACTOR -- improve code without breaking tests
public decimal ApplyDiscount(User user, decimal price)
{
    const decimal premiumThreshold = 100m;
    const decimal premiumDiscount = 0.20m;

    if (user.IsPremium && price > premiumThreshold)
        return price * (1 - premiumDiscount);
    return price;
}
// All tests still pass

When TDD genuinely helps me:

  • Business logic with many edge cases — discount rules, tax calculations, state machine transitions. TDD forces you to enumerate all the cases before writing code.
  • Fixing a bug — I write a test that reproduces the bug first. This proves I understand the bug AND guarantees it can never regress.
  • Designing an API — writing the test first forces you to think about how the API should feel to use, not how it's implemented internally. You end up with cleaner interfaces.
  • Core domain logic — anything in the domain layer is TDD-worthy.

When I don't do TDD:

  • CRUD endpoints with no business logic — the ORM handles most of it, integration tests are more valuable.
  • Infrastructure code (migrations, logging setup) — hard to unit test, integration tests are better.
  • Exploratory/spike code — write it first, then add tests once you know the shape of the solution.

The real benefit of TDD:

It's not about the tests themselves — it's that the practice forces you to design code that's testable. Testable code = loosely coupled, single-responsibility, well-defined interfaces. The test suite is a side effect of good design.

followUps.question: What is BDD (Behaviour Driven Development) and how is it different from TDD?
followUps.answer:
BDD extends TDD by writing tests in a language that non-technical stakeholders can read and verify. Instead of CalculateDiscount_PremiumUser_Returns20Percent(), BDD uses Given/When/Then notation:

Given a premium user, When they purchase items totalling over £100, Then they receive a 20% discount.

Tools like SpecFlow (C#) let you write these in Gherkin syntax and map them to C# step definitions. The feature file is literally the specification, written by the product owner or QA, and the code behind it is the automated test. BDD is valuable when there's a business analyst or QA who can write the scenarios — the developer implements the steps. For solo devs or small teams, it adds overhead without much benefit. TDD with descriptive test names achieves most of the same goal.

questions.tip: TDD is a design tool, not just a testing technique. If your code is hard to test, that's the code telling you something is wrong with the design — too many dependencies, too much responsibility, or tightly coupled to infrastructure.

questions.question: What is code coverage and how do you measure it in .NET? What is a good coverage target?
questions.answer:
Code coverage measures what percentage of your code lines are executed by tests. It's a useful indicator but easily misunderstood — high coverage doesn't mean good tests, and chasing 100% coverage is often a waste of time.

Measuring coverage in .NET:

# Run tests with coverage collection (using Coverlet)
dotnet test --collect:"XPlat Code Coverage"

# Generate HTML report (install ReportGenerator first)
dotnet tool install -g dotnet-reportgenerator-globaltool
reportgenerator \
    -reports:"**/coverage.cobertura.xml" \
    -targetdir:"coverage-report" \
    -reporttypes:Html
open coverage-report/index.html

In CI (GitHub Actions):

- name: Run tests with coverage
  run: dotnet test --collect:"XPlat Code Coverage"

- name: Upload coverage to Codecov
  uses: codecov/codecov-action@v3
  with:
    files: '**/coverage.cobertura.xml'

What to target — my honest opinion:

  • Domain/business logic — aim for 80-90%+. This is where bugs cost money. Every branch of a discount rule, every state transition, should be tested.
  • Application services — 70-80% is realistic. Mock heavy code.
  • Infrastructure (repositories, migrations, startup) — 30-50% is fine. Integration tests cover this better than unit tests.
  • Overall target — 70-80% is healthy for most projects. 100% is usually false confidence (tests written to boost coverage, not to catch bugs).

What coverage doesn't tell you:

// This has 100% line coverage
public decimal Divide(decimal a, decimal b) => a / b;

[Fact]
public void Divide_Works() => Assert.Equal(2m, Divide(10, 5));

// But what about Divide(10, 0)? DivideByZeroException.
// Coverage says 100% but the important case is untested.
// Branch coverage would catch this -- test both b!=0 and b==0 branches.

Line coverage says 'this line was executed'. It doesn't say 'this line was tested with the right assertions'. Focus on coverage of branches and edge cases, not just line count.

followUps.question: What is mutation testing and how is it different from code coverage?
followUps.answer:
Mutation testing automatically introduces small bugs (mutations) into your code — flipping a > to >=, changing a + to -, removing a condition — and checks whether your tests catch them. If a mutation survives (tests still pass), your tests are too weak. The percentage of killed mutations is the 'mutation score' — a much stronger measure of test quality than line coverage.

In .NET, Stryker.NET is the mutation testing tool. It's slower than regular tests but reveals the actual quality of your test suite. I run it on the core domain layer periodically, not on every CI build. Finding a 60% mutation score on a class you thought was 'well tested' is a humbling experience.

questions.tip: Coverage is a floor, not a ceiling. Use it to find uncovered code that probably should be tested. Don't use it as a KPI — teams that chase coverage numbers write pointless tests that assert things like Assert.NotNull(result) without any real business assertion.
